{{Short description|Branch of social psychology}}
{{more citations needed|date=March 2023}}
The '''psychology of social class''' is a branch of [[social psychology]] dedicated to understanding how social class affects individual's thoughts, feelings, and behaviors. While [[social class]] has long been a subject of analysis in fields such as [[sociology]], [[political science]], [[anthropology]], [[medicine]] and [[epidemiology]], its emergence within the field of [[psychology]] is much more recent.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Kraus|first1=Michael W.|last2=Stephens|first2=Nicole M.|date=September 2012|title=A Road Map for an Emerging Psychology of Social Class|journal=Social and Personality Psychology Compass|volume=6|issue=9|pages=642–656|doi=10.1111/j.1751-9004.2012.00453.x|issn=1751-9004}}</ref>

== Defining social class ==
[[Social class]] is often defined inconsistently, or not at all, within the social sciences.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Côté|first=Stéphane|date=January 2011|title=How social class shapes thoughts and actions in organizations|journal=Research in Organizational Behavior|volume=31|pages=43–71|doi=10.1016/j.riob.2011.09.004|issn=0191-3085}}</ref> Definitions tend to focus either on the essential properties of social class (i.e., conceptual definitions) or on how social class is measured (i.e., operational definitions). Conceptual definitions often define social class as a "cultural identity encompassing both a person's objective resources and their subjective rank relative to others.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|author1-link=Paul Piff|last1=Piff|first1=Paul K.|last2=Kraus|first2=Michael W.|author3-link=Dacher Keltner|last3=Keltner|first3=Dacher|date=2011|title=Social class, culture, and the convergence of resources and rank|website=PsycEXTRA Dataset|doi=10.1037/e634112013-188}}</ref>" Whereas operational definitions describe social class as "a reflection of one's social position, measured by income, education, and occupation.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last1=Adler|first1=Nancy E.|last2=Snibbe|first2=Alana Conner|date=August 2003|title=The Role of Psychosocial Processes in Explaining the Gradient Between Socioeconomic Status and Health|journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science|volume=12|issue=4|pages=119–123|doi=10.1111/1467-8721.01245|s2cid=16625770|issn=0963-7214}}</ref>" Additionally, the terms [[social class]] and [[socioeconomic status]] are often used interchangeably as both tend focus on an individual's material or economic resources and on their social position relative to others.

Definitions of social class also vary on the extent to which they emphasize objective or subjective elements of social class. Objective definitions tend to focus on income, education, and occupation and suggest that higher social class is attained through having more money, being more educated, and having greater occupational prestige than others.<ref name=":3" /> Alternatively, subjective definitions focus on individuals' perceived rank relative to others <ref name=":4"/> and suggest that higher social class is attained through one's belief that they rank higher than others because they "perceive that they have more money, a more advanced education, and/or a more prestigious occupation than others."<ref name=":1" />

While definitions of social class remain inconsistent, definitions in [[social psychology]] tend to focus on both the objective and subjective elements and most often define social class as a dimension of the self and/or a cultural identity "rooted in objective material resources and subjective perceptions of rank vis-a-vis others.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":4" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Kraus|first1=Michael W.|last2=Piff|first2=Paul K.|last3=Keltner|first3=Dacher|date=2009|title=Social class, sense of control, and social explanation.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=97|issue=6|pages=992–1004|doi=10.1037/a0016357|pmid=19968415|issn=1939-1315}}</ref>"

== Measurements ==
Similar to definitions of social class, measurements of social class tend to focus on the objective and/or subjective dimensions of class.

=== Objective measures ===
Measures of objective social class in psychology have primarily focused on education, income, and/or occupation.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Quirke|first=Linda|date=2010-01-25|title=Annette Lareau and Dalton Conley, eds., Social Class: How Does it Work?|journal=Canadian Journal of Sociology|volume=35|issue=1|pages=173–175|doi=10.29173/cjs7401|issn=1710-1123|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Oakes|first1=J.Michael|last2=Rossi|first2=Peter H|date=February 2003|title=The measurement of SES in health research: current practice and steps toward a new approach|journal=Social Science & Medicine|volume=56|issue=4|pages=769–784|doi=10.1016/s0277-9536(02)00073-4|pmid=12560010|issn=0277-9536}}</ref> [[Educational attainment]] is often viewed as a "gateway" to higher social class and therefore is frequently considered to be the most fundamental measure of social class.<ref name=":0" /> For example, advanced education leads to increased income.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Roworth|first=Wendy Wassyng|date=2002|title=Professional Ethics: Day by Day|journal=Academe|volume=88|issue=1|pages=24–27|doi=10.2307/40252115|jstor=40252115|issn=0190-2946 |url=https://digitalcommons.uri.edu/art_facpubs/14 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Pascarella|first1=Ernest T.|last2=Terenzini|first2=Patrick T.|title=Institutional Integration Scale|website=PsycTESTS Dataset|year=2020|doi=10.1037/t05853-000}}</ref> and access to professional networks.<ref>{{Cite book|last=RIVERA|first=LAUREN A.|title=Pedigree|date=2016-03-22|publisher=Princeton University Press|doi=10.2307/j.ctv7h0sdf|isbn=978-1-4008-8074-4}}</ref> Someone with a four-year college degree will earn twice as much money in their lifetime as someone with a high-school degree.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Domhoff|first1=G. William|last2=Ginzberg|first2=Eli|date=July 1998|title=New Deal Days: 1933-1934|journal=Contemporary Sociology|volume=27|issue=4|page=400|doi=10.2307/2655506|jstor=2655506|issn=0094-3061}}</ref> [[Personal income|Income]], however, provides the most direct assessment of people's access to material goods (e.g., food, clothing, and shelter) and also predicts an array of psychological variables such as well being,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Howell|first1=Ryan T.|last2=Howell|first2=Colleen J.|date=2008|title=The relation of economic status to subjective well-being in developing countries: A meta-analysis.|journal=Psychological Bulletin|volume=134|issue=4|pages=536–560|doi=10.1037/0033-2909.134.4.536|pmid=18605819|issn=1939-1455}}</ref> social trust,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Brandt|first1=Mark J.|last2=Wetherell|first2=Geoffrey|last3=Henry|first3=P. J.|date=2014-08-28|title=Changes in Income Predict Change in Social Trust: A Longitudinal Analysis|journal=Political Psychology|volume=36|issue=6|pages=761–768|doi=10.1111/pops.12228|issn=0162-895X|hdl=10411/20641|hdl-access=free}}</ref> personality,<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Piff|first=Paul K.|date=January 2014|title=Wealth and the Inflated Self: Class, Entitlement, and Narcissism|url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0146167213501699|journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin|language=en|volume=40|issue=1|pages=34–43|doi=10.1177/0146167213501699|pmid=23963971|s2cid=42266830|issn=0146-1672|url-access=subscription}}</ref> and [[prosocial behavior]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Robinson|first1=Angela R.|last2=Piff|first2=Paul K.|date=2017|title=Deprived, but not depraved: Prosocial behavior is an adaptive response to lower socioeconomic status|journal=Behavioral and Brain Sciences|volume=40|article-number=e341|doi=10.1017/s0140525x17001108|pmid=29342766|s2cid=10275632|issn=0140-525X}}</ref> One's [[Job (role)|occupation]] also provides important signals of social class. In psychology, occupation is most often measured in the form of [[occupational prestige]], or the admiration and respect given to a particular job in society. For example, jobs with higher occupational prestige tend to be more admired and respected within society, and are often held by those with high levels of educational attainment and usually come with higher salaries (e.g., lawyers, doctors). Alternatively, occupations with lower prestige tend to be less admired and respected in society, pay less money, and are frequently held by people who are less educated (e.g., construction workers, janitors).

=== Subjective measures ===
<!-- Deleted image removed: [[File:USladder.gif|thumb|479x479px|MacArthur Scale of Subjective Socioeconomic Status]] -->
While objective measures such as education, income, and occupation are important indices of social class, people's subjective perceptions of where they sit relative to others has been found to impact psychological functioning above and beyond objective measures.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Boyce|first1=Christopher J.|last2=Brown|first2=Gordon D.A.|last3=Moore|first3=Simon C.|date=2010-02-18|title=Money and Happiness|journal=Psychological Science|volume=21|issue=4|pages=471–475|doi=10.1177/0956797610362671|pmid=20424085|hdl=1893/12866|s2cid=520060|issn=0956-7976|hdl-access=free}}</ref> The [https://macses.ucsf.edu/research/psychosocial/subjective.php MacArthur Scale of Subjective Social Status]<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Adler|first1=Nancy E.|last2=Epel|first2=Elissa S.|last3=Castellazzo|first3=Grace|last4=Ickovics|first4=Jeannette R.|date=2000|title=Subjective SES Scale|website=PsycTESTS Dataset|doi=10.1037/t20985-000}}</ref> is the most widely-used measure of social class rank relative to others. In this measure, people are asked to rank themselves on a ladder with 10 rungs which represent ascending levels of income, education, and occupation:<ref name=":9">{{Cite journal|last1=Adler|first1=Nancy E.|last2=Epel|first2=Elissa S.|last3=Castellazzo|first3=Grace|last4=Ickovics|first4=Jeannette R.|date=November 2000|title=Relationship of subjective and objective social status with psychological and physiological functioning: Preliminary data in healthy, White women.|journal=Health Psychology|volume=19|issue=6|pages=586–592|doi=10.1037/0278-6133.19.6.586|pmid=11129362|s2cid=21584664 |issn=1930-7810}}</ref><ref name=":10">{{Cite journal|last1=Goodman|first1=E.|last2=Adler|first2=N. E.|last3=Kawachi|first3=I.|last4=Frazier|first4=A. L.|last5=Huang|first5=B.|last6=Colditz|first6=G. A.|date=2001-08-01|title=Adolescents' Perceptions of Social Status: Development and Evaluation of a New Indicator|journal=Pediatrics|volume=108|issue=2|article-number=e31|doi=10.1542/peds.108.2.e31|pmid=11483841|issn=0031-4005|doi-access=free}}</ref><blockquote>''"'''Think of this ladder as representing where people stand in the United States.'''''

''At the '''top''' of the ladder are the people who are the best off - those who have the most money, the most education, and the most respected jobs. At the '''bottom''' are the people who are the worst off - who have the least money, least education, and the least respected jobs or no job. The higher up you are on this ladder, the closer you are to the people at the very top; the lower you are, the closer you are to the people at the very bottom.''

'''''Where would you place yourself on this ladder?'''''

''Please place a large '''"X"''' on the rung where you think you stand at this time in your life, relative to other people in the United States'''"''''' </blockquote>This measure can also be easily adapted to reflect relative rankings among one's local community or subsets of society. For example, in place of ''"...in the United States"'' one could measure perceptions of subjective rank in one's community by replacing this text with ''"... in their communities"'' and asking them to place themselves on the ladder ''"relative to other people in their community."'' Research using both the local and societal level ladders demonstrates that subjective perceptions of one's social class relative to others are important and distinct dimensions of social class.<ref name=":9" /><ref name=":10" />

== Research ==

=== Social class as a cultural identity ===
In the first wave of research on the psychology of social class, social classes were conceived as a form of cultural identity. In this sense, individuals come to embody class-specific thoughts, feelings, and behaviors through learned norms, values, and expectations shared by others of similar social class backgrounds.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Snibbe|first1=Alana Conner|last2=Markus|first2=Hazel Rose|date=2003|title=Who gets to choose? Socieconomic status variability in the effects of choice on object liking|website=PsycEXTRA Dataset|doi=10.1037/e633872013-793}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kohn|first=Melvin L.|date=January 1963|title=Social Class and Parent-Child Relationships: An Interpretation|journal=American Journal of Sociology|volume=68|issue=4|pages=471–480|doi=10.1086/223403|s2cid=144214868|issn=0002-9602}}</ref> These norms, values, and expectations are then expressed through cultural practices such as food consumption, taste in art and music, language, clothing, and ways for expressing oneself and adjusting to others.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Stephens|first1=Nicole M.|last2=Markus|first2=Hazel Rose|last3=Townsend|first3=Sarah S. M.|date=2007|title=Choice as an act of meaning: The case of social class.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=93|issue=5|pages=814–830|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.93.5.814|pmid=17983302|issn=1939-1315}}</ref> The first theoretical account positing social class as a cultural identity<ref name=":4"/> contends that social class reflects more than just the material resources that individuals possess, and that objective resources shape individual's cultural practices and behaviors that signal social class. Individuals display their objective resources (e.g., educational attainment, family wealth/income, and occupational prestige) through class-related signals (e.g., symbols of wealth, education, or occupation, aesthetic preferences, and social behavior). Through these class-related signals, individuals provide the information needed to compare their own social class to that of others. These comparisons then separate people into different social class categories which become the basis for individual's subjective understanding of their social class rank vis-à-vis others and lead to different psychological and behavioral outcomes for low versus high social class individuals.

=== Conceptions of self ===
Supporting the idea that social class is a cultural identity, beyond differences in structural resources and individual skills, people from lower social class backgrounds also experience cultural barriers that maintain social-class disparities.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Stephens|first1=Nicole M.|last2=Townsend|first2=Sarah S. M.|last3=Dittmann|first3=Andrea G.|date=2018-12-18|title=Social-Class Disparities in Higher Education and Professional Workplaces: The Role of Cultural Mismatch|journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science|volume=28|issue=1|pages=67–73|doi=10.1177/0963721418806506|s2cid=149969990|issn=0963-7214|doi-access=free}}</ref> In the United States, different social class contexts foster different cultural models of self.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Markus|first1=Hazel Rose|last2=Kitayama|first2=Shinobu|date=July 2010|title=Cultures and Selves|journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science|volume=5|issue=4|pages=420–430|doi=10.1177/1745691610375557|pmid=26162188|s2cid=7533754|issn=1745-6916}}</ref> Due to fewer financial resources, greater environmental constraints, and fewer opportunities for choice, working-class or low social class contexts tend to foster an interdependent model of self.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last1=Kraus|first1=Michael W.|last2=Piff|first2=Paul K.|last3=Mendoza-Denton|first3=Rodolfo|last4=Rheinschmidt|first4=Michelle L.|last5=Keltner|first5=Dacher|date=2012|title=Social class, solipsism, and contextualism: How the rich are different from the poor.|journal=Psychological Review|volume=119|issue=3|pages=546–572|doi=10.1037/a0028756|pmid=22775498|issn=1939-1471}}</ref><ref name=":7">{{Cite journal|last1=Stephens|first1=Nicole M.|last2=Markus|first2=Hazel Rose|last3=Phillips|first3=L. Taylor|date=2014-01-03|title=Social Class Culture Cycles: How Three Gateway Contexts Shape Selves and Fuel Inequality|journal=Annual Review of Psychology|volume=65|issue=1|pages=611–634|doi=10.1146/annurev-psych-010213-115143|pmid=24079532|issn=0066-4308|doi-access=free}}</ref> To effectively navigate these contexts, low-social class individuals must rely on and work together with others for material assistance and support. In contrast, middle-class or higher-social class contexts provide greater access to economic capital and more opportunities for choice,<ref name=":8">{{Cite journal|last1=Stephens|first1=Nicole M.|last2=Fryberg|first2=Stephanie A.|last3=Markus|first3=Hazel Rose|last4=Johnson|first4=Camille S.|last5=Covarrubias|first5=Rebecca|date=2012|title=University Administrators' Institutional Expectations Task|website=PsycTESTS Dataset|doi=10.1037/t33052-000}}</ref><ref name=":6" /> fostering an independent model of self. To be effective in these contexts they must learn to influence others, challenge the status quo, and express their own personal interests.

In addition to shaping cultural models of self, socioeconomic status (SES) also influences [[self-concept]] stability. Research has shown that lower-SES individuals tend to adapt to situational demands and have more context-dependent self-concepts, while higher-SES individuals experience greater autonomy forming a more stable self-concept that remains consistent across different contexts.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Boucher |first=Helen C. |date=2021-04-03 |title=Social class and self-concept consistency: Implications for subjective well-being and felt authenticity |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15298868.2020.1726443 |journal=Self and Identity |volume=20 |issue=3 |pages=406–422 |doi=10.1080/15298868.2020.1726443 |issn=1529-8868|url-access=subscription }}</ref> This stability has been linked to higher well-being through observed greater [[life satisfaction]] and lower anxiety.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Boucher |first=Helen C. |date=2021-04-03 |title=Social class and self-concept consistency: Implications for subjective well-being and felt authenticity |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15298868.2020.1726443 |journal=Self and Identity |volume=20 |issue=3 |pages=406–422 |doi=10.1080/15298868.2020.1726443 |issn=1529-8868|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Although both models of self can be highly useful, U.S. gateway institutions (e.g. higher education) tend to prioritize independence as the cultural ideal.<ref>Markus, H. R. and Connor, E. 2013. ''Clash! 8 cultural conflicts that make us who we are'', New York, NY: Hudson Street Press.</ref> In higher education, arguably one of the most important gateways to [[social mobility]], administrators and educators often enact an independent model of self when making assumptions about how students should be motivated, learn, and interact with others.<ref name=":7" /> Students are expected to express their personal preferences, pave their own paths, and challenge norms and rules.<ref name=":8"/> Research suggests that when people from working class backgrounds enter institutions that prioritize independence, they face a cultural mismatch. Experiencing cultural mismatch can lead people from working class backgrounds to feel uncomfortable enacting the independent behaviors required to gain access to gateway institutions. For example, students from working class backgrounds are unlikely to apply to selective universities,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Hoxby|first1=Caroline|last2=Avery|first2=Christopher|date=December 2012|title=The Missing "One-Offs": The Hidden Supply of High-Achieving, Low Income Students|location=Cambridge, MA|doi=10.3386/w18586|doi-access=free|hdl=1903/24593|hdl-access=free}}</ref> feel uncomfortable separating themselves from their families and communities,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Covarrubias|first1=Rebecca|last2=Fryberg|first2=Stephanie A.|date=2015|title=Family Achievement Guilt Questionnaire|website=PsycTESTS Dataset|doi=10.1037/t43171-000}}</ref> and are more reluctant to pursue paths to organizational power when doing so requires self-interested behavior.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Laurin|first=Kristin|date=January 2014|title=Research and Interventions for Empowering Members of Historically Disadvantaged Groups|journal=Academy of Management Proceedings|volume=2014|issue=1|page=11923|doi=10.5465/ambpp.2014.11923symposium|issn=0065-0668}}</ref> Even when these students do gain admission to these institutions, the cultural mismatch that they face can impede on their opportunity to succeed. When individuals' cultural norms are not included in institutions, they feel uncomfortable and less often perform up to their potential.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Brannon|first1=Tiffany N.|last2=Markus|first2=Hazel R.|last3=Jones-Taylor|first3=Valerie|date=2012|title=Two Souls, Two Thoughts, Two Self-Schemas: Positive Consequences of Double-Consciousness|website=PsycEXTRA Dataset|doi=10.1037/e521512014-312}}</ref><ref name=":8" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Stephens|first1=Nicole M.|last2=Townsend|first2=Sarah S.M.|last3=Markus|first3=Hazel Rose|last4=Phillips|first4=L. Taylor|date=November 2012|title=A cultural mismatch: Independent cultural norms produce greater increases in cortisol and more negative emotions among first-generation college students|journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology|volume=48|issue=6|pages=1389–1393|doi=10.1016/j.jesp.2012.07.008|issn=0022-1031}}</ref> Additionally, displaying interdependent behaviors such as humility, instead of independent behaviors such as confidence, leads to more negative performance evaluations.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kennedy|first1=Jessica A.|last2=Anderson|first2=Cameron|last3=Moore|first3=Don A.|date=November 2013|title=When overconfidence is revealed to others: Testing the status-enhancement theory of overconfidence|journal=Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes|volume=122|issue=2|pages=266–279|doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2013.08.005|s2cid=11934267|issn=0749-5978}}</ref>

=== Relating to others ===
Conceptions of self also elicit class-specific ways of relating to others. The interdependent norms of low social class contexts tend to engender greater social responsiveness which leads people from lower social class backgrounds to more accurately understand others' emotions and to engage in more pro-social behavior. For example, higher class individuals tend to demonstrate more signs of disengagement and fewer signs of engagement than their lower class counterparts during interactions with strangers (CITE Kraus & Keltner, 2009). Lower social class individuals also tend to perceive people's emotions more accurately<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kraus|first1=Michael W.|last2=Côté|first2=Stéphane|last3=Keltner|first3=Dacher|date=2010-10-25|title=Social Class, Contextualism, and Empathic Accuracy|journal=Psychological Science|volume=21|issue=11|pages=1716–1723|doi=10.1177/0956797610387613|pmid=20974714|s2cid=7306762|issn=0956-7976}}</ref> because they pay greater attention to contextual cues. Further, people with lower household incomes tend to donate a higher proportion of their salary to charities than do those from higher income households and are more likely to behave pro-socially toward others.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Piff|first1=Paul K.|last2=Kraus|first2=Michael W.|last3=Keltner|first3=Dacher|date=2011|title=Social class, culture, and the convergence of resources and rank|website=PsycEXTRA Dataset|doi=10.1037/e634112013-188}}</ref> Research further suggests that compared to higher social class individuals, lower social class individuals have more egalitarian values, are more likely to help a stranger in distress, and trust others more.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Piff|first1=Paul K.|last2=Kraus|first2=Michael W.|last3=Côté|first3=Stéphane|last4=Cheng|first4=Bonnie Hayden|last5=Keltner|first5=Dacher|date=2010|title=Having less, giving more: The influence of social class on prosocial behavior.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=99|issue=5|pages=771–784|doi=10.1037/a0020092|pmid=20649364|issn=1939-1315}}</ref> Conversely, people who are perceived as being of lower social class are less likely to receive help from others compared to those perceived as being of higher social class.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Callaghan | first1=Bennett | last2=Delgadillo | first2=Quinton M. | last3=Kraus | first3=Michael W. | title=The influence of signs of social class on compassionate responses to people in need | journal=Frontiers in Psychology | date=2022 | volume=13 | article-number=936170 | doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2022.936170 | pmid=36092048 | pmc=9455153 | doi-access=free }}</ref>

== Relationship to other constructs ==

=== Power and status ===
Recent research has sought to distinguish social class from other dimensions of [[hierarchy]], such as [[Power (social and political)|power]], or a "person's relative control over resources and ability to influence others,<ref name=":11">{{Cite journal|last1=Magee|first1=Joe C.|last2=Galinsky|first2=Adam D.|date=January 2008|title=8 Social Hierarchy: The Self-Reinforcing Nature of Power and Status|journal=The Academy of Management Annals|volume=2|issue=1|pages=351–398|doi=10.1080/19416520802211628|issn=1941-6520}}</ref> and [[Social status|status]], or "one's level of respect and admiration from others.<ref name=":11" />" Although interrelated, empirical evidence suggests that social class, measured both objectively and subjectively, is not reducible to power or status and that the correlations between social class and power and status are small to moderate.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Anderson|first1=Cameron|last2=Kraus|first2=Michael W.|last3=Galinsky|first3=Adam D.|last4=Keltner|first4=Dacher|date=2012-05-31|title=The Local-Ladder Effect|journal=Psychological Science|volume=23|issue=7|pages=764–771|doi=10.1177/0956797611434537|pmid=22653798|s2cid=8406753|issn=0956-7976}}</ref>

Piff et al. (2012) found that upper-class individuals engage in more unethical behavior than lower-class individuals for a number of reasons. Factors such as their relative independence from others, increased privacy, fewer structural constraints, more resources to successfully deal with the potential fallout, and feelings of entitlement may all contribute to the findings that higher class is correlated with higher rates of wrongdoing.<ref>Piff, P.K., Stancato, D.M., Côté, S., Mendoza-Denton, R., and Keltner, D. (2012). Higher social class predicts increased unethical behavior. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 109, 4086–4091.doi:10.1073/pnas.1118373109.</ref>

=== Social class and mental health ===
Social class can have a significant impact on mental health. It is generally accepted that there is a higher concentration of mental illnesses among those from lower social classes.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=Murali|first1=Vijaya|last2=Oyebode|first2=Femi|date=May 2004|title=Poverty, social inequality and mental health|journal=Advances in Psychiatric Treatment|language=en|volume=10|issue=3|pages=216–224|doi=10.1192/apt.10.3.216|issn=1355-5146|doi-access=free}}</ref> Many suggest that this is due to increased stressors of living in a lower class. Examples of these stressors include an unstable place of residence, food insecurity, lack of access to important resources, debt, etc.<ref name=":13">{{Cite journal|last1=Simon|first1=Kevin M.|title=Addressing Poverty and Mental Illness|url=https://www.psychiatrictimes.com/view/addressing-poverty-and-mental-illness|access-date=2021-06-05|website=Psychiatric Times|series=Vol 35, Issue 6 |date=29 June 2018 |volume=35 |last2=Michaela |first2=Beder }}</ref> Economic inequality can directly affect the quality of care patients receive as well as the accessibility of the care. The [[economic inequality]] individuals face causes these issues in many ways including, inability to afford to travel to a mental health clinic, work hours overlapping with clinic hours, etc.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Hodgkinson|first1=Stacy|last2=Godoy|first2=Leandra|last3=Beers|first3=Lee Savio|last4=Lewin|first4=Amy|date=January 2017|title=Improving Mental Health Access for Low-Income Children and Families in the Primary Care Setting|journal=Pediatrics|volume=139|issue=1|pages=e20151175|doi=10.1542/peds.2015-1175|issn=0031-4005|pmc=5192088|pmid=27965378}}</ref> More in depth information on this topic can be found at [[Socioeconomic status and mental health|socioeconomic_status_and_mental_health#external_link]] .

=== Race, ethnicity, and gender ===
Social class has been related to other status-based social categories that also effect conceptions of self and how individuals relate to others. For example, similar to being low social class, being female (compared to being male) tends to promote more interdependent norms for relating to others<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Cross|first1=Susan E.|last2=Madson|first2=Laura|date=1997|title=Elaboration of models of the self: Reply to Baumeister and Sommer (1997) and Martin and Ruble (1997).|journal=Psychological Bulletin|volume=122|issue=1|pages=51–55|doi=10.1037/0033-2909.122.1.51|issn=1939-1455}}</ref> and lower status racial minorities (i.e., African Americans) tend to exhibit more relational norms compared to racial majority members (i.e., European-Americans).<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Boykin|first1=A. Wade|last2=Jagers|first2=Robert J.|last3=Ellison|first3=Constance M.|last4=Albury|first4=Aretha|date=1997|title=Communalism Scale|website=PsycTESTS Dataset|doi=10.1037/t21394-000}}</ref> Class, race, and gender also have similar effects on individuals sense of belonging in academic institutions.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Steele|first1=Claude M.|last2=Aronson|first2=Joshua A.|date=2004|title=Stereotype Threat Does Not Live by Steele and Aronson (1995) Alone.|journal=American Psychologist|volume=59|issue=1|pages=47–48|doi=10.1037/0003-066x.59.1.47|pmid=14736323|issn=1935-990X}}</ref> Students from lower social class backgrounds tend to experience increased anxiety about confirming to negative stereotypes about their social class when a test is framed as diagnostic of ability, and inevitably perform worse as a result.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Croizet|first1=Jean-Claude|last2=Claire|first2=Theresa|date=June 1998|title=Extending the Concept of Stereotype Threat to Social Class: The Intellectual Underperformance of Students from Low Socioeconomic Backgrounds|journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin|volume=24|issue=6|pages=588–594|doi=10.1177/0146167298246003|s2cid=145536011|issn=0146-1672}}</ref> This parallels research on the role of [[stereotype threat]] in the performance of racial minorities and women in academic settings.

Further, social class is largely intertwined with the mental representations of other categories of identity (i.e., race). For example, Black people are often stereotyped as unintelligent, lazy, and dishonest,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Devine|first1=Patricia G.|last2=Elliot|first2=Andrew J.|date=November 1995|title=Are Racial Stereotypes Really Fading? The Princeton Trilogy Revisited|journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin|volume=21|issue=11|pages=1139–1150|doi=10.1177/01461672952111002|s2cid=145214090|issn=0146-1672}}</ref> while White people are stereotyped as intelligent, motivated, and productive.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Dasgupta|first1=Nilanjana|last2=McGhee|first2=Debbie E|last3=Greenwald|first3=Anthony G|last4=Banaji|first4=Mahzarin R|date=May 2000|title=Automatic Preference for White Americans: Eliminating the Familiarity Explanation|journal=Journal of Experimental Social Psychology|volume=36|issue=3|pages=316–328|doi=10.1006/jesp.1999.1418|issn=0022-1031}}</ref> A further analysis of identity-based stereotypes reveals a direct overlap between the stereotypes associated with being Black and being poor (e.g., unintelligent),<ref name=":12">{{Cite journal|last1=Durante|first1=Federica|last2=Tablante|first2=Courtney Bearns|last3=Fiske|first3=Susan T.|date=March 2017|title=Poor but Warm, Rich but Cold (and Competent): Social Classes in the Stereotype Content Model|journal=Journal of Social Issues|volume=73|issue=1|pages=138–157|doi=10.1111/josi.12208|issn=0022-4537}}</ref> and those associated with being White and being rich (e.g., competent).<ref name=":12" /> These stereotypes play a pivotal role in how people interpret and categorize individuals. For example, observers are more like to categorize an ambiguously raced person as Black when the individual is wearing low-status clothing and as White when the individual is wearing high-status clothing.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Freeman|first1=Jonathan B.|last2=Penner|first2=Andrew M.|last3=Saperstein|first3=Aliya|last4=Scheutz|first4=Matthias|last5=Ambady|first5=Nalini|date=2011-09-26|title=Looking the Part: Social Status Cues Shape Race Perception|url= |journal=PLOS ONE|volume=6|issue=9|article-number=e25107|doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0025107|pmid=21977227|pmc=3180382|bibcode=2011PLoSO...625107F|issn=1932-6203|doi-access=free }}</ref> Additionally, mental representations of low social class people tend to be of Black people, whereas mental representations of high social class people tend to be of White people.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Brown-Iannuzzi|first1=Jazmin L.|last2=Dotsch|first2=Ron|last3=Cooley|first3=Erin|last4=Payne|first4=B. Keith|date=2016-11-24|title=The Relationship Between Mental Representations of Welfare Recipients and Attitudes Toward Welfare|journal=Psychological Science|volume=28|issue=1|pages=92–103|doi=10.1177/0956797616674999|pmid=27879320|s2cid=36034428|issn=0956-7976}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lei|first1=Ryan F.|last2=Bodenhausen|first2=Galen V.|date=2017-04-05|title=Racial Assumptions Color the Mental Representation of Social Class|url= |journal=Frontiers in Psychology|volume=8|page=519|doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2017.00519|pmid=28424651|pmc=5380730|issn=1664-1078|doi-access=free}}</ref>
